title: Victim compensation scheme - an underused remedy in criminal cases
p1:
Most people think justice ends with a conviction.
But what if I told you, in many criminal cases, victims are entitled to money from the state—and rarely claim it?
p2: A common misconception is that compensation only comes after long civil lawsuits or from the offender directly. In reality, courts in criminal cases can activate victim compensation schemes where the state steps in to provide financial support, especially when the accused is unable to pay. What’s surprising is that many victims never even get informed that this option exists, so the remedy quietly goes unused.
p3: Victim compensation schemes are government-backed funds meant to support survivors of crime with medical bills, rehabilitation, or financial loss. In many cases, courts or legal services authorities can recommend compensation during or after trial, sometimes even without a formal request. The key insight is that relief doesn’t always depend on the offender’s ability to pay. A simple application through the District Legal Services Authority can open a faster path to support than most people expect.
conclusion: How many victims miss out on rightful compensation simply because no one tells them this option exists during the legal process?
